game_name: Might and Magic: The Secret of the Inner Sanctum

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Foundational system; player selects from 6 classes (Knight, Cleric, Robber, Sorcerer, Paladin, Archer) and 5 races (Human, Elf, Half-orc, Gnome, Dwarf)
Core navigation system; turn-based movement on maze-like grid with 90-degree cardinal turns
Expansive terrain traversal; player navigates castles and open world without guided rails
Transition trigger from exploration to combat; introduces encounter unpredictability
Speed stat determines action order; includes options: attack, spell cast, run, surrender, bribe
Available to Sorcerer and Archer classes; damage-focused; limited by Intelligence-based spell points
Available to Cleric and Paladin classes; healing/defense/poison removal; limited by Personality-based spell points
Underground dungeon traversal; presents increased encounter density and disorientation risk
Complex interior environments; site-specific mechanics (e.g., Portsmith gender-based hazards)
Alien NPCs introduce Sheltem plot; VARN acronym decryption (Vehicular Astropod Research Nacelle); narrative twist
Central antagonist introduced as escaped prisoner masquerading as King; drives late-game objectives
Metaphysical realm; likely endgame location with high difficulty
Core objective; location unknown; requires unmasking Sheltem and resolving his machinations
End sequence portal; leads to Cron (Might and Magic II setting); reveals Sheltem escapes

rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based pacing allows player-controlled tension modulation; exploration is deliberate and cautious; combat encounters provide discrete tension peaks; mid-game sci-fi revelation shifts from medieval fantasy to cosmic horror, heightening psychological tension

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Maze-like grid structure with branching paths; some areas (e.g., Portsmith hazard zones) reward careful navigation and gender swapping for complete exploration
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Science fiction elements hidden within fantasy framework (crashed spaceship, aliens, VARN acronym decryption); alignment and gender mechanics have minimal overt signaling but unlock conditional events

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low; character creation establishes baseline stats; initial outdoor exploration and weak random encounters teach grid navigation and basic combat without severe punishment
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate to High; cave and underground city exploration introduces increased encounter density and disorientation; Sheltem revelation escalates narrative stakes and enemy difficulty
difficulty_curve.late_game: High; Astral Plane and Inner Sanctum require mastery of all combat systems, spell management, and likely feature elite encounters; final confrontation with Sheltem is climactic
notable_spikes: Transition from outdoor to cave systems (density spike)
Sheltem reveal at crashed spaceship (narrative + difficulty)

notable_level_patterns.description: Castles and towns serve as safe hubs with NPCs, quests, and alignment-checking. Players radiate outward into wilderness/dungeons, then retreat to hub for healing, spell point replenishment, and quest advancement
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Portsmith (underground city hub with gender-based trap zones)

notable_level_patterns.description: Crashed spaceship site introduces science fiction context and alien NPCs, fundamentally reframing the fantasy setting and introducing true antagonist (Sheltem); signals genre shift mid-campaign
Game progresses through nested spatial domains: outdoor Varn → castles/towns → caves/underground → Astral Plane → Gates to Another World; each layer increases isolation and difficulty
